title: Advanced Problem-Solving Patterns (Capstone)
description: A recognition guide tying every technique from Two Pointers through Dynamic Programming together -- not new syntax, but the map for choosing the right tool when facing a new problem.
objectives: Recognize which pattern a new problem resembles, based on its shape and constraints
See how a single problem can sometimes be solved with more than one pattern
Build a mental checklist for approaching an unfamiliar DSA problem

sections.text: 1. Why This Lesson Has (Almost) No New Code

Every technique needed is already covered -- Two Pointers, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Hashing, Binary Search, Recursion, Backtracking, Greedy, and Dynamic Programming. The hardest real-world skill isn't knowing an algorithm exists -- it's recognizing WHICH one applies to a brand new problem you've never seen. This lesson is that recognition guide.

sections.title: Problem signals -> likely technique
rows: A SORTED array, and pairs/triples summing to a target
Two Pointers (from opposite ends)
A CONTIGUOUS subarray/substring, min/max/count over all windows
Sliding Window (fixed or variable size)
MANY range-sum queries on a fixed array
Prefix Sum
MANY range-update operations, read final result only
Difference Array
"Have I seen this before?" / counting occurrences / pair lookups
Hashing (HashMap/HashSet)
A SORTED array, searching for a value or boundary
Binary Search
Exploring ALL possible choices, with the ability to abandon invalid paths
Backtracking
Repeatedly making the 'best right now' choice, with a PROVABLE greedy property
Greedy
Overlapping sub-problems, and asking 'what's the best/count/way' up to some state
Dynamic Programming (memoization or tabulation)
Tree/graph structure, visiting nodes level by level
BFS
Tree/graph structure, diving deep down one path first
DFS
Checking bits directly, or a single unpaired/unique value among duplicates
Bit Manipulation

sections.text: 3. Worked Example: Recognizing Multiple Valid Approaches
Take the pair-sum problem: given an array and a target, does any pair sum to it? We've now seen THREE different ways to approach variants of this exact question across this course.

sections.title: Choosing between them
points: If the array is ALREADY sorted (or sorting it is acceptable/cheap), Two Pointers uses less extra memory
If the array is UNSORTED and sorting isn't desired, Hashing solves it in one pass without reordering anything
Both beat brute force's O(n^2) -- recognizing this is more valuable than memorizing one 'correct' answer

sections.text: 4. Worked Example: A Problem That Combines Patterns

Some real problems blend multiple techniques. Consider: 'find the length of the longest substring with all unique characters.' This is fundamentally a SLIDING WINDOW problem (contiguous substring, window grows/shrinks) COMBINED with HASHING (a Set tracking characters currently in the window).

This is Sliding Window's variable-size expand/shrink structure, using a HashSet (Hashing) to check membership in O(1) instead of scanning the current window every time. Recognizing that TWO patterns combine here -- rather than treating this as some entirely new, unfamiliar problem -- is exactly the skill this capstone lesson is building.
5. A Practical Approach to a New Problem

sections.title: A mental checklist when facing an unfamiliar problem
points: What's the input shape? (array, string, tree, graph, sorted or not?)
What's actually being asked? (existence, count, minimum/maximum, all possibilities?)
Does brute force work, and what's ITS complexity? (always a valid, if slow, starting point)
Does the problem's shape match any pattern from the table above?
Could two patterns combine, like Sliding Window + Hashing here?
If nothing fits cleanly, can Dynamic Programming decompose it into overlapping sub-problems?

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to memorize solutions to specific problems instead of recognizing the underlying PATTERN -- patterns transfer to new problems, memorized solutions don't.
Assuming every problem has exactly ONE correct approach -- many have multiple valid solutions with different trade-offs (like pair-sum's Two Pointers vs Hashing).
Jumping straight to a complex technique (DP, backtracking) before checking if a simpler pattern (two pointers, sliding window, hashing) already solves it more directly.
Not writing the brute force solution first, even mentally -- it's often the fastest way to understand the problem correctly before optimizing.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: When solving an unfamiliar DSA problem, what should you check before choosing an approach?
questions.answer: The input's shape (sorted array, string, tree, graph?), what's actually being asked (existence, count, min/max, all possibilities?), whether a brute-force approach works as a starting baseline, and whether the problem's shape matches a known pattern -- possibly more than one combined.

questions.question: Give an example of a problem that can be solved with more than one valid pattern.
questions.answer: Pair-sum: Two Pointers works in O(n) time and O(1) space if the array is sorted; Hashing works in O(n) time and O(n) space on an unsorted array without needing to sort first -- both are valid, with different trade-offs.

questions.question: How can Sliding Window and Hashing combine to solve a single problem?
questions.answer: In problems like 'longest substring with all unique characters,' a sliding window expands and shrinks over a contiguous range, while a HashSet tracks which characters are currently inside the window, giving O(1) membership checks instead of rescanning the window each time.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: The real skill in DSA problem-solving is pattern recognition, not memorization: matching a new problem's shape (sorted array? contiguous range? tree/graph? overlapping sub-problems?) to a known technique from Two Pointers through Dynamic Programming. Many problems have multiple valid approaches with different trade-offs -- pair-sum solves via Two Pointers (sorted, O(1) space) or Hashing (unsorted, O(n) space) -- and some problems combine two patterns at once, like Sliding Window plus Hashing for longest-unique-substring. A practical approach: identify the input shape and the actual question being asked, start from brute force as a baseline, then look for a matching (or combined) pattern to optimize.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Don't memorize ANSWERS -- memorize the SHAPE each pattern solves. Sorted + pair-sum = Two Pointers. Contiguous + window = Sliding Window. 'Have I seen this?' = Hashing. Overlapping sub-problems = DP. Match the shape, not the specific problem.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What questions should you ask yourself when facing an unfamiliar DSA problem?
Name two different valid patterns for solving pair-sum, and their trade-offs.
How do Sliding Window and Hashing combine in the longest-unique-substring problem?
Why is pattern recognition more valuable than memorizing individual solutions?
